clean_story:
Falling water. Sky’s recycling system.

"Rain" comes from the Old English regn, meaning "water falling in drops"—a name that mirrors its action like a leaky bucket emptying itself.

Rain begins when water vapor cools into droplets around dust or salt, clinging like dewdrops to spider silk. Too heavy to float, they fall—but not all make it. Tiny droplets evaporate midair (virga), vanishing like breath on glass. Those that survive hit at 20 mph, slower than hail but fast enough to carve canyons over millennia.

A single storm dumps 1 million tons of water per second—enough to fill 400 Olympic pools in a minute. Yet some rains never touch ground: Sahara’s "rain shadows" steal moisture before it lands, while Mumbai’s monsoons drop 3 feet in a day, turning streets into rivers.

Three related subjects are clouds’ hidden weights, deserts stealing storms, and how puddles predict weather.
